title: Inspecting a beehive in summer
subtitle: Frame by frame, looking for a queen, a laying pattern, and the early signs of swarming.
excerpt: Summer is the working season for a colony. A full inspection takes around forty minutes, walks every frame from one wall of the brood box to the other, and answers four questions: is the queen here, is she laying well, is the colony preparing to swarm, and is there enough room above for the next two weeks of honey. Done weekly through May, June, and July; less often when the flow eases.
sourceNotes:
Cross-referenced from current UK beekeeping references: • National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), section 4 on routine inspections. Https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
• British Beekeepers Association, Bee Craft technical data sheets on swarm control and queen identification.
• Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive. Foundational UK primer; the rhythm of the inspection has changed little.
• Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (Marston House, 5th ed. 2010), referenced but not quoted; the seven-question approach to inspection derives loosely from Hooper's checklist.

content.text: Summer is the working season for a honey-bee colony in the UK. The queen is laying close to her seasonal peak, fifteen hundred eggs a day in a strong hive, and the colony is building wax, drawing comb, and putting honey above the brood box. By June it has the population to swarm, and through May, June, and July a beekeeper goes through every frame once a week to keep the swarm impulse in check and the supers under-filled rather than over-filled.

A full summer inspection takes around forty minutes for a single hive. It answers four questions, in this order: is the queen here, is she laying a healthy pattern, is the colony preparing to swarm, and is there enough room above for the next fortnight. Everything else rides along on the same visit: disease checks,
varroa
load, water access.

attrs.body: Pick a still, warm day, air temperature above 15°C, light wind, sun out. Midday to mid-afternoon is the slot when the most foragers are out of the hive, which means fewer bees on the frames and a quieter inspection. Don't inspect in the rain, after dusk, or in cold snaps; you'll chill the brood and the colony will hold a grudge for the next two visits. Once-a-week through May, June, and July is the rhythm; drop to fortnightly in August as the flow eases.
attrs.title: When to do this

content.text: What you need

items.name: Bee smoker, plus fuel (hessian or pine needles)
Full bee suit with integrated veil

content.text: Veil-only kit and a long-sleeved shirt is acceptable for experienced beekeepers on short midday checks of a calm colony. New beekeepers wear the full suit. A
smoker

that goes out partway through an inspection is the single most common reason a calm hive turns defensive, overload the fuel cup, get a steady column of cool white smoke, and check it three or four times during the visit.
Setting up at the hive
Light the
smoker

ten minutes before you reach the hive, a half-lit smoker working under pressure is more trouble than no smoker at all. Suit up at the gate, not at the hive, and walk in from the side rather than across the flight path. Standing in front of a hive entrance puts you in the route every returning forager is using; they will fly into you and then complain about it.

When you reach the hive, give two or three soft puffs of smoke at the entrance and wait sixty seconds. The smoke triggers the alarm response that sends workers to fill their crops with honey; a bee with a full crop is harder to bend into a sting position. Lift the roof, set it upside down a metre from the hive, and rest the crown board across it. Two more puffs through the crown board opening, and another wait.
Going through the supers
Crack each
super
loose with the
hive tool
, the joint will be sealed with
propolis

. Lift it as a single block off the box below and set it on the upturned roof. A full National super weighs around fifteen kilos when wet with honey; if the lift is uncomfortable, take it off one frame at a time instead.

Heft is the quick check on each super: full and uniform across both ends means honey on every frame; uneven heft means a few empty frames the bees haven't worked yet. Lift the corner with the hive tool, a quick judgement is enough. The detailed honey-frame check is the harvest job, not the weekly inspection.
The brood box, frame by frame
With the
queen excluder
lifted clear, the brood box is open. Start at the outermost
frame

on the side away from you, that one is usually stores rather than brood, so the queen is unlikely to be on it. Lift it straight up with two hands on the lugs, rest it on the hive stand or a frame perch, and start working inward.
On each frame from then on, four things to check:
Eggs. Look down into the bottom of the cells; a single white grain standing upright in the middle of a cell is an egg laid in the last three days. Eggs mean a queen has been here recently.
Larvae. C-shaped white grubs lying in cells, swimming in royal jelly. Open
brood
at different sizes is the sign of a queen laying continuously.
Capped brood. Even, slightly raised tan-yellow cappings across a large oval block in the middle of the
comb
. Patchy, sunken, or perforated cappings warrant a closer look, but on a routine inspection, a uniform pattern is the cue to move on.
Queen cells. Run a thumb along the bottom edge of every frame. A peanut-sized vertical
queen cell
hanging off the bottom edge is a
swarm cell
and changes today's plan; one or two on the face of a comb is
supersedure
and is the colony quietly replacing its queen.

Spotting the queen herself on every visit is a nice-to-have, not a must-have. Eggs prove she was here within the last three days, and that is the working answer to question one. If you do see her, mark the apiary card with the date and frame number. It tells you next week whether she has moved.

attrs.body: A solid central oval of capped brood, around the size of a side plate, with concentric rings of open larvae, then eggs, then pollen, then a final ring of honey around the edge. Holes in the pattern (skipped cells) of more than a handful per frame, or capped brood with sunken or perforated cappings, suggest a problem worth coming back to with the National Bee Unit factsheets. Don't try to diagnose mid-inspection; finish the visit, then read up.
attrs.title: What a healthy laying pattern looks like

content.text: Room above

The fourth question is the easiest. When the top super has more than two-thirds of its frames worked out and partially filled with nectar, add another super above it before next week's visit. A colony that runs out of room above is a colony looking for somewhere else to put the honey, and that pressure feeds into the swarm impulse. Easier to give two empty supers and watch them fill slowly than to leave one super crowded and find
Apis mellifera
hanging from your neighbour's apple tree on Saturday morning.
Closing up

Replace frames in the order they came out and the orientation they came out in, bees navigate by the position of frames in the box, and shuffling the order disrupts the rhythm. Settle the queen excluder back square, supers in their original order, crown board, roof. Give one last soft puff at the entrance, walk away, and write the visit up on the apiary card while it's fresh: date, weather, queen seen / not seen, eggs yes / no, queen cells yes / no, room added, anything else worth noting.
What you might have found

attrs.intro: Four things that change the plan for the next week.

items.fix: Do swarm control today, the artificial-swarm method or a Demaree split. Do not just knock the cells off and walk away; the colony will rebuild them within three days and swarm anyway.
items.cause: The colony has decided to swarm within the next week to ten days. The trigger is some combination of population, congestion, and queen age.
items.symptom: Swarm cells on the bottom edge of one or more frames, with eggs or young larvae inside.

items.fix: Leave the cells alone. Mark the date on the apiary card and check again in ten days; expect to see the old queen gone and a young virgin or newly mated queen taking over.
items.cause: Supersedure. The colony is quietly replacing a failing or ageing queen without swarming.
items.symptom: One or two queen cells on the face of a comb, queen still laying well.

items.fix: Mark the date, check again in three days for emergency cells. If none appear, add a frame of eggs and young larvae from another hive, the colony will draw a queen from those. Avoid panic-buying a replacement queen on day one.
items.cause: Queenless, recently. The colony hasn't yet started emergency queen cells.
items.symptom: No eggs, no young larvae, only sealed brood; bees calm but not building.

items.fix: Add another super above, and either move a couple of central nectar frames up into the super or extract them. The queen needs room to lay; a honey-bound brood box is one of the leading causes of summer swarming.
items.cause: Strong flow has pushed nectar into the brood box because there isn't enough space above.
items.symptom: Honey-bound brood box, frames of nectar pressing in on the central brood area, queen running out of laying space.

attrs.heading: Common summer-inspection findings

content.text: The varroa load check, the disease scan, and the swarm-control routine sit alongside the inspection rather than inside it. They are separate tutorials, used as the findings call for them. The weekly visit on its own is enough to keep most colonies healthy and most beekeepers grounded.
